ResiWalk Field Inspection App — Training Guide
Field Inspection Platform
ResiWalk
How to use the app end to end — inspections, the camera & AI tools, scoping, review, approval, and reporting.
Audience: Internal team & 1099 contractors
Version: June 2026
ResiHome / ResiWalk Confidential — do not distribute outside authorized partners.

Contents

This guide walks through ResiWalk end-to-end — from signing in to finalizing and reporting. Internal staff and 1099 contractors use the same app; sections that differ by role are called out. Screens shown throughout are from the live app.

1Getting Started — Install & Sign In2The Home Screen3Scheduling & Assigning Inspections4Inspection Types at a Glance5The Camera & Media Tools6The 1099 Leasing Agent Inspection7The Scope Rate Card Inspection8Adding Lines by Voice & Camera AI9The Final Checklist10AI Review (before you submit)11Submitting, Approval & Hand-off12The Turn Reinspection13Reports & PDFs14Good to Know15Scope Approval16Reviewing & Finalizing17ReInspect Fail Alerts18MIRD Alerts & Updates19Turn Reinspect Pass Notifications20Vacancy & 1099 Inspection Automation21The 1099 Agent Review Process22Get Into the Home — Rently Access Codes23PPW Recurring Services — Grass Fails
The Big Picture
ResiWalk turns a property walk into a structured, photo-backed record. You inspect, the app validates your entries with AI, a manager approves, and the system auto-generates the reports, emails the team, opens a maintenance ticket, and pushes tenant chargebacks to the ledger — no manual paperwork.
1

Getting Started — Install & Sign In

Install the App

  • Android: Go to resiwalk.com/install and add the app to your home screen.
  • iPhone / iPad: Open resiwalk.com in Safari, tap Share, then “Add to Home Screen.”
  • Anywhere: You can also just use resiwalk.com in a browser.

Sign In

  • Internal Team: Enter your Resi email and confirm with your Google account.
  • 1099 Contractors: Sign in with Google or Microsoft — whichever matches your email.
Secure & Simple
Everyone signs in with their work email — no separate password. Only active users can sign in; deactivated accounts are blocked automatically.
Sign in with Google (staff) or Microsoft (1099).
2

The Home Screen

The home screen is your inspection pipeline — every open inspection in one list.

Find What You Need

  • Filter: by inspector or by template (inspection type).
  • Sort: by date or by community; flip the sort direction.
  • Status Toggles: All, Scheduled, In Progress, Pending Approval, Completed — each with a live count.
  • Paging & Search: search by address/name/inspector, and page through results.
  • Completed: open any completed inspection to review it and download its PDFs.
The pipeline: search, status counts, filters, and inspection cards.
3

Scheduling & Assigning Inspections

Inspections are created from the property record — most details fill themselves in.

Auto-Populated from HubSpot

Region, property status, bedroom & bathroom counts, and the scheduled date all pull straight from HubSpot — no re-typing.

Before You Start

  1. 1Pick the inspection type (template) and the property.
  2. 2Reschedule, or reassign to a different user (active staff only).
  3. 3Tap Begin Inspection to go straight in.
Active Users Only
The assignee list shows active users only — you can’t assign an inspection to someone who has been deactivated.
New Inspection — template, property, beds/baths, date & inspector pre-filled.
4

Inspection Types at a Glance

ResiWalk supports several templates; the app shows the right questions and tools for each.

  • 1099 Leasing Agent: Guided photo & question walk — listing health, condition, HVAC, meters, trash.
  • Scope Rate Card: The full turn scope — line items, quantities, vendors, pricing, tenant chargebacks.
  • Turn Reinspect QC: Verify a vendor completed the scoped work, line by line, against the before photos.
  • Community Visit: Confirm a community looks right and everything is in shape.
  • Vacancy / Occupancy Check: A quick drive-out to confirm whether a home is vacant or occupied.
  • New Construction RQC: Readiness / quality-control report for built-to-rent and construction hand-off.
Same Tools Everywhere
Every template uses the same camera, markup, voice, and validation tools — learn one inspection and you know them all.
5

The Camera & Media Tools

Photos and video are the backbone of every inspection. The in-app camera does a lot.

Capturing

  • Two Levels: add photos to a line item, or to a whole section/room.
  • Photos & Video: tap the shutter (rapid-fire for fast shots); use the video toggle to record clips, which save alongside your photos.
  • Location-Stamped: stamps address, time, GPS, and on-site vs. off-site distance.
  • Lenses & Zoom: pinch/drag to zoom; tap a lens chip for a sharper lens (remembered).
  • Flash: switch to the phone’s native camera when you need the flash.
  • Tap to Focus: tap anywhere on the frame — even while recording video.

Review & Mark Up

  • Swipe: move through photos and video clips together in one viewer.
  • Mark Up: annotate with arrows, circles, or a pen in brand colors.
  • Built-In Gallery: pull in an existing photo right from the camera — no separate upload.
  • Tag to a Line Item: open a captured photo and choose the line item to attach it to.
In-app camera with location badge & lens chips.
Markup: arrows & circles on the photo.
Tap a photo to tag its line item.
6

The 1099 Leasing Agent Inspection

A guided walk that captures the home’s condition and listing health. The header shows type, status, listing price, and how long it’s been listed — plus your pass/fail tally and “all changes saved.”

What You’ll Cover

  • Listing Feedback: evaluate listing price (Reduce / Keep / Increase) and lead feedback (Good / Fail).
  • Action Required: a Fail prompts a required note and optional photos so the issue is documented.
  • Whole House, HVAC & Utilities: device hubs, exterior & interior photos, HVAC components, filters, meter numbers, trash bins.

Answer the questions, capture the required photos, then Submit. The progress count (e.g. “8/19 answered”) shows what’s left.

A question with pass/fail, required note, and photos.

Utilities & Trash Bins → Auto Compliance Ticket

On a Leasing Agent (1099) inspection, the Final Checklist captures the home’s Utilities — Electric, Gas, and Water — plus the Trash Bins. If a utility is switched OFF, or the trash bins come back Missing, ResiWalk automatically opens a ticket for the Utilities team the moment you submit. No re-keying, nothing to chase.

  • One ticket per issue: Electric Off, Gas Off, Water Off, and Trash Bins Missing each open their own ticket, so every outage is tracked and closed on its own.
  • Record the Meter # carefully: when a meter is OFF, the Meter Number you enter is written onto the ticket — shown in bold red — so the Utilities team can begin their research immediately. Double-check it before you submit.
  • The right photo, attached automatically: the meter photo from that line item is pulled straight from the inspection and attached to the ticket — the Water ticket carries the water-meter photo, the Gas ticket the gas-meter photo, and so on.
  • Property linked for you: the home from the inspection is automatically associated to the ticket, so the team has the full property record one click away.
  • Lands in Compliance Issues: the ticket is created in the Compliance Issues pipeline at the New stage, ready for triage.
  • Named for instant triage: the ticket name calls out the issue, the utility type, and the address — e.g. 1099-Inspection - Water Off - 123 Main St, Dallas, TX, 75201 — so the team spots an outage or a missing bin at a glance.
Off or Missing = An Instant Utilities Ticket
Flip Electric, Gas, or Water to OFF (or mark Trash Bins Missing) and submit — ResiWalk opens a Compliance Issues ticket for each issue, names it by utility and address, drops in the Meter Number (in bold red) and the matching meter photo, and links the property. The Utilities team picks it up in the New stage with everything they need to start.

Evaluate Listing Price → Asset Management Alert

Every Leasing Agent inspection asks you to Evaluate Listing Price — Reduce, Keep, or Increase. If you pick Reduce or Increase, a Recommended New Monthly Rent is required (plus a note explaining why). On submit, that recommendation is routed straight to Asset Management in the #listing-price-reductions Slack channel — with AI-validated comps — so a pricing decision can be made fast.

  • Reduce / Increase needs a number: choosing Reduce or Increase requires the Recommended New Monthly Rent and a note. (Keep needs nothing further.)
  • Slack alert on submit: if a pricing recommendation was made, submitting the inspection posts to #listing-price-reductions for Asset Management.
  • The live numbers, side by side: the alert shows the property’s current live list price, the agent’s recommended new rent (with the dollar change), and the agent’s note.
  • AI validates in real time: the app pulls market data and posts 3 active comparable rentals with matching data points (rent, $/sqft, days on market, distance) plus an AVM range and a one-line read on whether the request is supported — so the team can confirm or push back.
  • Comps live in the thread: the main message stays compact and says “Comparable homes in thread”; tap replies to see the comps. Each comp address links out for a closer look.
Reduce / Increase = An Instant Pricing Review
Pick Reduce or Increase, enter the recommended rent + a note, and submit — ResiWalk posts the live list price, your recommendation, and 3 AI-validated comps to #listing-price-reductions so Asset Management can act in minutes.

Pool Condition → PPW (Recurring Services) Review

When a home has a pool, the Leasing Agent inspection adds a Pool Condition line item (shown only when the property has a pool fee on file). Mark it Pass or Fail. A Fail requires a note describing what’s wrong and a photo of the pool — then, on submit, it’s routed straight to the PPW (Recurring Services) team to investigate.

  • Only when there’s a pool: the Pool Condition question appears automatically for properties that carry a pool fee — no pool, no question.
  • Fail = note + photo: choosing Fail opens an Action Required block that requires a written reason (“What is wrong with the pool?”) and a photo showing its condition.
  • Sent to PPW on submit: a failed pool review posts to the PPW (Recurring Services) team in Slack — with the address, inspector, note, and photo — so they can act.
  • PPW investigates & reports back in HubSpot: once they review, PPW logs their feedback as a Note on both the Property record and the Active Listing, so the update lives where the team works.
  • The right people are tagged: each update @-mentions the Property Manager and the Listing Agent so they’re notified the moment PPW responds.
Pool Fail = An Instant PPW Review
Fail the Pool Condition, add a note + photo, and submit — ResiWalk alerts PPW (Recurring Services). They investigate and post their feedback as a Note on the Property and Active Listing, tagging the Property Manager and Listing Agent.
7

The Scope Rate Card Inspection

The full turn scope and the financial heart of the app — you build the work room by room and it prices it.

Build the Scope

  • Sections (Rooms): add, rename, or delete rooms inline; the settings menu’s Manage Sections also lets you reorder (drag) and clear a room’s lines & photos — by room or in bulk (Clear All).
  • Line Items: pick a category, sub-category, and item; set quantity; choose the vendor; adjust the vendor price or tenant bill-back %.
  • Auto-Proration: the tenant chargeback is automatically prorated by how long the tenant lived there.
  • Internal Resolution: assign a line to “Internal Resolution” for work your team completes in-house (with a Now/Later choice and after-photos).

See the Money Before You Submit

  • Totals Strip: Lines, Vendor, Client, Tenant, and Net Turn update live at the top.
  • Drill-Down: “Expand all” breaks costs out by category and line item across Vendor, Tenant, and Net.
Validate the Chargeback
Before finalizing, confirm the tenant total lines up with what you expect — the drill-down makes it a quick check.
Add a line — category, qty, vendor, tenant %, live pricing.
Expand-all drill-down by category, with the live totals strip.
Manage Sections: drag to reorder, rename, clear, or delete rooms.

Eviction Vendor & the Scope → HoneyBadger Tickets

When you assign a Scope line to a vendor, that choice controls how the work is packaged into HoneyBadger tickets at finalize. Two pieces are new:

1) Eviction Vendor — Work Timing

The eviction trash-out is now a single “Eviction Vendor” option. When you pick it, choose the Eviction Work Timing:

  • Previously Completed — the work was already done. Kept as a historical record only — no vendor packet and no eviction ticket.
  • Needs Completed — the work is still owed. Generates a vendor packet and a separate Eviction ticket in HoneyBadger.

2) Scope → HoneyBadger Ticket Automation

A single Scope finalize can now create up to three tickets — each carrying only its own documents:

  • Turnkey ticket — the standard trades + the Master report. (Flagged as a $0 Turn when there’s no turnkey work.)
  • Evictions ticket (Evictions type) — the “Needs Completed” eviction packet. New tickets use the Trash/Debris Removal category, Medium priority, Whole House.
  • CapEx ticket (Maintenance type) — the CapEx Vendor packet.
One Scope, the Right Tickets
Assign each line to its vendor and set the eviction timing — ResiWalk splits the finalize into the Turnkey, Evictions, and CapEx tickets automatically, each with just its own packet. “Previously Completed” eviction work is recorded but never re-ticketed.

Download the Reports After Finalize (Tenant & Vendor PDFs)

Once a Scope is finalized/approved, the report PDFs live right on the inspection — you can re-download them any time (e.g. to attach the tenant charges to a SODA). On the completed Scope header, tap “View PDFs ▾” and pick the document:

  • Master Report — the full scope report. View.
  • Tenant Chargeback (PDF) — the tenant charges. View.
  • Vendor — [Vendor] — each vendor’s packet (one row per vendor). View.
  • Tenant Chargeback Import (xlsx) — the spreadsheet for import. Tap the ↓ to download.

To download: tap View to open the PDF, then use your device’s share/download to save or send it; for the xlsx, tap the download arrow. Nothing is lost after approval — the reports stay available on the record.

Need a copy again? It’s already there.
Open the completed Scope → View PDFs → pick Master, Tenant Chargeback (PDF), a Vendor packet, or the Tenant Chargeback Import (xlsx). Re-download as many times as you need.
8

Adding Lines by Voice & Camera AI

You don’t have to tap through menus — talk to it, or point the camera and describe what you see. (Look for the mic and AI buttons in the scope footer.)

Voice Assistant

  1. 1Tap the microphone at the bottom of the scope.
  2. 2Say the work plainly — e.g. “clean whole house, level one sales clean,” or “go to the kitchen and replace the microwave.”
  3. 3It finds the matching catalog line, prices it, and reads back what it added. The mic stays open so you can reply or add the next item without tapping again.

Camera AI

Open the AI camera and describe (or scan) the issue — it suggests the right line item in real time. Great for capturing a defect and scoping the fix in one motion.

Speak Naturally
List several things in one breath (“the yard needs leaves raked and a gutter cleaning, 50 linear feet, two-story”) — it splits them into separate lines and asks if it needs a quantity.
9

The Final Checklist

At the bottom of the scope, a closing checklist makes sure the essentials are captured. (The 1099 inspection has a similar checklist.)

  • Bluetooth Lock: mark online/offline and record the serial number.
  • Universal Garage Remotes: if missing, the app offers to add the line — choose “Add line” or “Not needed.” Added lines appear back at the top.
  • Mailbox: same add-line prompt if missing.
  • HVAC: confirm it’s functioning; if not, you can be prompted to add a service/clean line.
  • Major Appliances + Label Stickers: photograph the label/serial stickers — feeds warranty tracking.
  • Air Filters: capture filter details for the Second Nature delivery program.
  • Final Notes: add anything else about the inspection.
Prompts Catch Misses
The checklist actively prompts you to add the right line when something’s missing — so remotes, mailbox keys, and HVAC service don’t slip through.
10

AI Review (before you submit)

Before a Scope Rate Card can be submitted, it runs an AI Review — a second set of eyes on your scope.

What It Checks

  • Logic: do the selected line items make sense together?
  • Duplicates: flags work scoped twice (e.g. cleaning a carpet you’re replacing).
  • Missing Info: prompts you to add a photo, or to justify a missing cleaning or painting scope — so nothing’s skipped.

You’re in Control

For each suggestion you can Edit, Add, or Ignore it — in real time. Nothing changes without you.

Green Means Go
Once you’ve worked through it, the review turns green and Submit unlocks — a quick pass keeps quality high before a manager sees it.
11

Submitting, Approval & Hand-off

Submitting a scope kicks off an approval and automation chain — what used to be manual now happens for you.

The Flow

  1. 1You submit, and the inspection moves to Pending Approval.
  2. 2A regional manager or director reviews the line items and photos and confirms they match before dispatch.
  3. 3They hit Finalize.

What Finalize Does Automatically

  • Generates the PDFs: master, vendor, and tenant chargeback.
  • Emails Them: to the SODA box.
  • Creates a Turnkey Ticket: in Honeybadger MM — documents attached, under Unit Turns — for the MC to dispatch.
  • Pushes the Charge Import: to the ledgers for tenant chargebacks. Issues route a second email to the SODA box for PM Accounting.
Two Sets of Eyes
The person who submits a scope can’t finalize their own — a second reviewer must approve it. Every turn is double-checked.
12

The Turn Reinspection

After the vendor completes the work, create a reinspection to verify it — quickly, against the original scope.

How It Works

  1. 1Create a Turn Re-Inspect QC. It defaults to the most recently submitted scope and copies every line with its “before” photo.
  2. 2Go line by line: see the before photo, then validate the work — a quick Pass / Fail.
  3. 3Take the “after” photo for each line as proof.
  4. 4A Fail requires a note explaining what happened and what’s needed.
  5. 5Give the overall inspection a Pass / Fail at the end.

The overall result determines whether the property goes On-Market or stays Under Turnkey until failed lines are done. On submit, the maintenance coordinator is notified in real time.

Reinspect: before/after strips, per-line pass/fail, required fail note.
13

Reports & PDFs

Every finalized scope produces a clean set of PDFs, downloadable from the completed inspection.

The Documents

  • Master Report: page 1 is a summary of every line item; following pages break it down by room and section.
  • Per-Vendor PDFs: and the Internal Resolution PDF — full transparency for each vendor and in-house work.
  • Tenant Chargeback: PDF and the charge import (xlsx).

Clickable Photos

Every photo in a PDF is clickable — it opens the actual photo gallery to swipe through. These are public links vendors can open directly.

Chargebacks Are Automatic
On finalize, the charge import file is pushed to the ledgers automatically. Any issue routes to the SODA box for PM Accounting to correct.
Download any document — or all six — from the completed inspection.
The Master report: line-item summary, then photo galleries by room.
14

Good to Know

A few things working quietly in the background that make ResiWalk reliable in the field.

Autosave

You never hit “save.” Every answer, line, and photo saves as you work; offline, changes queue and upload the moment you reconnect.

Offline-First Photos

Photos and clips are stored on the device and sync in the background — a dead zone never blocks the walk or loses a capture.

Audit Trail

Each inspection keeps a history under its menu — who submitted, approved, reopened, cancelled, and edited it, and when. Edits log once per session (and again after you leave and return), so the record stays clear without noise.

Updates

When a new version ships, a banner offers a one-tap reload. The app otherwise updates itself in the background.

If Something Looks Off
Reload from the update banner (or fully close and reopen the app) to be sure you’re on the latest version before reporting an issue.
15

Scope Approval

When you submit a Scope Rate Card, the approval moves to Slack automatically — routed to the right people, with the money already on the card.

Where It Lands

  • Auto-Routed by Region: on submit, the scope posts straight into your region’s #ratecard channel — no forwarding, no tagging, no chasing.
  • One Source of Truth: the message is the request. The reviewers in that channel see everything they need to act.

What’s in the Alert

  • Property & Submitter: the full address and who submitted the scope.
  • Ratecard Total & Tenant Total: the vendor-facing total and the tenant chargeback, surfaced up front.
  • Approval Routing: the thresholds that decide who has to sign off (below).
  • Two Buttons: View Inspection in HubSpot opens the deal record; Review Inspection opens the live scope in ResiWalk.
Approval Thresholds
Exceeding $1,500 requires approval from Jo Ann Haynes. Exceeding $5,000 requires approval from the Director. The alert names the approver so it never sits in limbo.
Scope ApprovalAPP7:10 PM
Scope Rate Card review needed: Test Property 7, Dallas, TX, 30319
Requesting a Scope review for: Test Property 7, Dallas, TX, 30319
Submitted By: Eric Williams
Ratecard Total: —
Tenant Total: —
Exceeding $1,500 will require APPROVAL from @Jo Ann Haynes. Exceeding $5,000 will require approval from Director.
View Inspection in HubSpot Review Inspection
A new scope in #ratecard. The pink action bar means approval is still required.
16

Reviewing & Finalizing

The reviewer opens the scope from Slack, confirms it in ResiWalk, and finalizes — and the same Slack message updates itself to show it’s done.

The Approval Flow

  1. 1From the #ratecard alert, tap Review Inspection to open the live scope inside ResiWalk.
  2. 2Check the line items, photos, and totals against the property — the same review a manager runs in section 11.
  3. 3Hit Finalize. That fires the full automation chain — PDFs, SODA email, the Honeybadger turnkey ticket, and the charge import.

Read the Action Bar

  • Pink bar = approval still required. The scope is waiting on a reviewer — it has not been finalized yet.
  • Blue bar = approval complete. Once it’s finalized, the original Slack message rewrites itself in place — the bar turns blue and it shows Approved By and Approved At.
  • No duplicate posts: the channel updates the existing card rather than stacking new messages, so the thread stays clean.
Two Colors, One Glance
You never have to open a thread to know where a scope stands. Pink = act on it. Blue = handled. Scan the channel and the colors tell the story.
Scope ApprovalAPP9:41 PM
Approved: 65 Sonya Cir, Covington, GA, 30016
APPROVED: 65 Sonya Cir, Covington, GA, 30016
Submitted By: asanders
Approved By: Hayden Woods
Approved At: 2026-06-12 22:00 UTC
View Inspection in HubSpot Review Inspection
1 replyJun 12th at 6:07 PM
Same message, now finalized. The blue action bar — plus Approved By and Approved At — confirms it’s done.
17

ReInspect Fail Alerts

A completed Turn Reinspection fires its own Slack notifications — and a Fail escalates straight to the people who need to move on it.

A Fail Routes to the Pod

  • Pod-Specific #PM-OPS: when a reinspect comes back Fail, the alert lands in that pod’s #PM-OPS channel and notifies everyone watching it.
  • The Call to Action: a Fail means the vendor has to go back — the notification flags that the home needs to be re-dispatched.
  • Master Report Attached: the failing scope’s PDF is linked right on the card so the whole pod sees what failed.

It Pulls the Schedule for You

  • Current Move-In Ready Date: the card reads the property’s live MIRD so everyone sees the date the work is racing against.
  • Future Move-Ins: it reviews open Applications and Deals — if a resident is lined up, the card surfaces it. (Full MIRD / Lease Start Date alerting is in section 18.)
Everyone Sees It at Once
The Fail notification puts the address, the failed work, the MIRD, and any pending lease in front of the whole pod in one post — so re-dispatch happens fast and nothing waits on a hand-off.
Scope ApprovalAPP2:27 AM
Turn ReInspect Fail
2329 Sw 103Rd Ter, Oklahoma City, OK, 73159
Submitted By: Richard Jones
Current Move-In Ready Date: Jun 17, 2026
Pending Lease — Lease Start Date: Jun 15, 2026
This property has a Submitted Application | Pending Lease. Please Prioritize.
• Deal Status: Move-in Scheduled — Lease Start Date: Jun 15, 2026
PDF — Master Report URL: Open report
Update MIRD Review Inspection
A reinspect Fail in the pod’s #PM-OPS channel — with the MIRD, the pending lease, and a one-tap Update MIRD.
18

MIRD Alerts & Updates

The Fail notification doesn’t just report — it watches the calendar and tells you when the Move-In Ready Date needs a second look.

When You’ll Be Warned

  • MIRD Past or Within 3 Days: if the current Move-In Ready Date is already behind us — or lands within three days — the notification adds an alert telling you to update the MIRD.
  • Upcoming Lease Start Date: if there’s a Submitted/Approved Application or a Pending Deal, the card surfaces the upcoming Lease Start Date — driving vendor urgency and flagging that the LSD may need review.

Update the MIRD from Slack

  1. 1Tap Update MIRD on the notification — right where you saw the alert.
  2. 2You’re prompted to supply a new estimated MIRD and an optional note.
  3. 3On submit, the Listings team is notified automatically in the #listing-mird-updates channel so the listing stays accurate.
Urgency, Built In
By tying the reinspect Fail to live applications, deals, and dates, the alert turns “a line failed” into “a resident is moving in on the 15th” — so the right people prioritize the right home.
Scope ApprovalAPP2:29 AM
@Laura — Move-In Ready Date update requested
Property: 2329 Sw 103Rd Ter, Oklahoma City, OK, 73159
Current MIRD: Jun 17, 2026
Requested New MIRD: Jun 22, 2026
Requested by: @Eric Williams
Note: TEST - COMMENT BOX - NO ACTION
This home had a QC / ReInspect Fail. Please review and update the Move-In Ready Date.
Open Property
1 replyJun 11th at 9:04 AM
Tapping Update MIRD posts the requested new date and notifies the Listings team in #listing-mird-updates.
19

Turn Reinspect Pass Notifications

A Pass is the green light — submitting one says the home is out of Turn and ready to list. ResiWalk now fires that hand-off to the Listings team automatically, the moment you submit.

Where It Lands

  • Auto-Routed to Listings: on a Pass, the notification posts in real time to your POD’s #listing-requests channel — routed by region (e.g. -ga, -florida, -scattered, -west) — and tags the Listings owner. No forwarding, no chasing.
  • One Source of Truth: the card carries the address, submitter, current MIRD, and any pending lease — Listings sees everything they need to act in a single post.

What It Triggers

  • Update the Move-In Ready Date: the alert tells Listings to set the MIRD to the current date — the home is move-in ready now.
  • Validates Open Deals & Pending Move-Ins: the card checks live Deals and pending move-ins; if a pending deal is found it surfaces the Deal Stage and the Lease Start Date (when provided).
  • No PM Action Needed: this is a clean hand-off to Listings — nothing for the PM team to do.
Green Means List It
A green Pass card is the signal a home is ready for market. Listings is already notified and already has the MIRD and pending-lease context — so the home moves from Turn to On-Market without a hand-off.

20

Vacancy & 1099 Inspection Automation

Vacancy and 1099 Leasing Agent inspections now route themselves on what they find — a clean pass files itself, and an action item opens a ticket. No re-keying.

A Clean Pass Files Itself

  • Passed / No Action Items: if a Vacancy or 1099 report comes back PASSED or with no action items, it’s stored in HubSpot for reporting — no ticket created, nothing to chase.

An Action Item Opens a Ticket

  • Auto Work Order in HoneybadgerMM: a 1099 report with Call-To-Actions or Fails automatically generates a Work Order in HoneybadgerMM — a Maintenance ticket with the Category set to Inspection.
  • Failed Items in the Description: every flagged or failed item from the 1099 inspection is written into the ticket Description, so the reviewer sees exactly what was called out.
  • Report Attached: a copy of the 1099 inspection is saved to the ticket’s Documents — full context, one click away.
Pass = File It  •  Fail = Fix It
Nothing falls through. A clean report becomes a record in HubSpot; an action item becomes a maintenance ticket the moment it’s submitted — pre-categorized, described, and with the report attached.
Ticket Details NEW
Property113 Cotton Boll Drive, Madison, AL 35757
StatusNew
Property StatusVacant - On Market
Ticket TypeMaintenance

21

The 1099 Agent Review Process

The review you already run hasn’t changed — auto-generated inspection tickets land on the On-Market tab in Power BI, ready for your call.

Where to Review

  • Power BI → On-Market Tab: 1099 Agent-documented requests show up here. New tickets generated from 1099 reports land in this view for review.
  • The New Tkts KPI: the KPI box calls out how many Inspection tickets are still in NEW status — your queue at a glance.
  • The Tables Below: new and open existing 1099 inspection tickets are tracked in the New 1099 WOs and Open 1099 WOs tables.

Make the Call

  • Open & Review: the PM, APM, MC, Field Technician, and PM team open the ticket and review the flagged potential work.
  • Approved → Dispatch: if the work is approved, the PM team generates the Work Order and advises the Maintenance Coordinator to dispatch a vendor.
  • Not Approved → Cancel: if the work isn’t needed, the PM team simply cancels the ticket. Done.
New = Your Queue
Every auto-generated inspection ticket lands in NEW. Clear the NEW count and you’ve reviewed every flagged home — approve to dispatch, or cancel to close it out.

The On-Market tab in Power BI — the New Tkts KPI flags your queue, with new and open 1099 inspection tickets tracked in the tables below.
22

Get Into the Home — Rently Access Codes

Need to get into the home? You don’t have to leave ResiWalk to do it. Every active inspection now pulls a one-tap access code straight from Rently.

One Tap to Unlock

  • The Unlock Circle: every active inspection now has an unlock-icon circle in the top-right corner — right next to Back. Tap it and your access code pops up. No app-switching, no calling in for a code.
  • Works with Smart Locks: Rently codes work for SmartHome setups with Hubs, Smart Bolts, and Blue locks.
  • Valid for ~1 Hour: the code is good for about an hour from first use — plenty of time to get in and complete the walk.

Entering the Code

  • Hubs: enter the code as shown — you’re in.
  • Smart Bolts & Blue Locks: these can need a checkmark before and after the code — e.g. checkmark + code + checkmark. If the code alone doesn’t open the door, bracket it with the lock’s checkmark / enter key.

Every Request Is Logged

  • Full Audit Trail in Rently: every code request is logged in Rently with your name, the inspection / work-order number, and the date & time — so access stays accountable and traceable.
No More App-Switching
Request, unlock, inspect — all without leaving ResiWalk. One tap on the unlock circle and you’re in.

active inspection

23

PPW Recurring Services — Grass / Landscape Fails

When a 1099 Leasing Agent Inspection (Section 6) flags grass or landscape out of spec, ResiWalk routes the failure — with photos — straight to the PPW Recurring Services team in Slack, who investigate and report findings back without leaving the channel.

How It Triggers

  • Documented on the walk: during a 1099 Leasing Agent Inspection the agent marks Grass Condition = “Fail – Needs Attention” and backs it up with photos.
  • Auto-routed on submit: the moment the inspection is submitted, the report and photos are sent to the PPW Recurring Services team automatically — nobody has to forward anything.
  • Posted to Slack: ResiWalk drops a notification directly in #1099-agent-ppw-fails with the address, inspector, the inspection note, and a side-by-side photo strip.
Channels
# general
# 1099-agent-ppw-fails
# scope-approval
The alert lands in #1099-agent-ppw-fails.
ResiWalkAPP12:20 PM
@PPW a 1099 Agent Inspection has been submitted and the Grass/Landscape review has failed. Please review PPW and submit feedback.
245 Helen Ln, Stockbridge, GA, 30281
Inspector: Adelina Paloji
Note: Front and back grass need to be cut
View ReportLeave Note on Property
Added by ResiWalk
A pink-bar alert with the photo strip, a green View Report button, and a red Leave Note on Property button.

PPW Investigates & Reports Back

  1. 1Review the photos and the inspector’s note in the alert. Tap View Report for the full ResiWalk inspection.
  2. 2Click Leave Note on Property. A pop-up opens showing the property details with an Update / findings box.
  3. 3Type what you found / what is being done, then click Save to HubSpot.

What the Note Does

  • Writes to the Property: your update is saved as a note on the Property record and the Property Manager (from the property’s manager field) is tagged.
  • Writes to a Published Listing: if the property has a published listing, the same note is added to the Listing and the current Listing Agent is tagged.
  • One source of truth: the inspection details, the report link, and your findings all live on the record — no separate email thread to chase.
On the HubSpot record
The note reads “Grass / Landscape review FAILED … PPW update: <your findings>” and tags @Property Manager on the property and @Listing Agent on the listing.

Closing the Loop — Pink Turns Blue

As soon as the note is saved, ResiWalk updates the original Slack alert: the bar changes from pink to blue, your update is shown, and the buttons are removed so it can’t be actioned twice. The whole channel can see at a glance that the fail has been handled.

ResiWalkAPP12:35 PM
✅ Note added to HubSpot + 1/1 listing.
245 Helen Ln, Stockbridge, GA, 30281
Inspector: Adelina Paloji
Update: Contacted vendor — mowing scheduled Friday.
Updated in HubSpot by eric.williams
Once handled, the alert recolors blue, shows the PPW update, and drops the buttons.
At a Glance
Pink = needs PPW attention. Blue = investigated and noted on the property/listing. No spreadsheet, no forwarding — the color of the alert tells the whole story.
